one_line_takeaway: Agent-BOM is a hierarchical attributed-graph representation (static capability layer + runtime semantic layer + typed edges + security attributes) plus a path-template auditing paradigm (entry, backward trace, forward trace, adjudication) that, demonstrated via four qualitative OpenClaw case studies mapped to the OWASP Agentic Top 10, reconstructs multi-stage agent attack chains (memory poisoning to tool misuse, supply-chain skill hijack to code execution, multi-agent goal hijack cascades, and rogue-agent trust/privilege abuse) end-to-end, but offers no quantitative detection performance and depends entirely on instrumentation completeness.
key_finding: Across all four composite case studies, the Agent-BOM graph plus the four-stage rule-based pipeline (entry localization, backward tracing, forward tracing, adjudication) was able to reconstruct the full attack chain end-to-end -- linking the untrusted entry point (external input, malicious skill, tampered inter-agent message, or rogue system prompt) through semantic-state evolution (context, reasoning, decision) to the realized impact (workspace deletion, data exfiltration, cascading task drift, unconfirmed email send) -- and to correctly attribute each phase to the corresponding OWASP Agentic Top 10 category. The paper also presents a qualitative comparison (Table II) of representation families (SBOM, AIBOM, Logging, Tracing, Observability, generic/agent-workflow/reasoning/security-oriented provenance) against six derived auditing questions Q1-Q6, using a checkmark/triangle/cross rubric (direct support / partial support / no support) rather than measured scores, concluding that Agent-BOM is the only family with 'High' representation-layer portability and 'High' overall auditing support among the compared families.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Agent-BOM (Agent Bill of Materials), a hierarchical attributed directed graph representation, formally B_S = (A, V, E, alpha), for security auditing of LLM agent systems. It separates a static capability layer V_static (models, tools, prompts, skills, code, long-term memory) from a runtime semantic layer V_runtime (external inputs, goals, context, reasoning, decisions, actions, observations), connected by four edge families (structural dependency, runtime evolution, cross-layer binding, cross-agent propagation edges) and a security attribute mapping alpha (source, trust_level, integrity_status, authorization, confirmation_status, environment_change, etc.). On top of this schema the authors define a path-level auditing rule template R = <v_entry, P_back, P_fwd, C> (audit entry, backward tracing, forward tracing, adjudication conditions) and instantiate it as ten concrete rules mapped to the OWASP Agentic Top 10 for 2026 (ASI01-ASI10, e.g. Goal Hijack, Tool Misuse, Identity & Privilege Abuse, Supply Chain Vulnerabilities, Unexpected Code Execution, Memory & Context Poisoning, Insecure Inter-Agent Communication, Cascading Failures, Human-Agent Trust Exploitation, Rogue Agents). They implement an auditing plugin for the OpenClaw agent runtime (v2026.2.6, Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS, GPT-5 backbone) that builds an Agent-BOM graph from live executions in four steps (static-layer extraction, runtime-layer extraction from logs, instrumentation-based dependency recovery, graph assembly with attributes), and runs a four-stage rule-based auditing pipeline (entry localization, backward tracing, forward tracing, adjudication).
threat_model: In-scope: application-layer risks in agentic workflows expressible as paths over agent execution states, capability objects, and their relations -- goal hijacking via prompt injection, poisoned-RAG-induced semantic drift, persistent memory contamination, identity/privilege abuse, unauthorized capability invocation. Adversary: can inject malicious payloads or deceptive semantics via user inputs, retrieved external documents, third-party tool outputs, shared context, and agent-to-agent messages; injected content may lie dormant in memory/reasoning before triggering downstream effects. Trusted computing base: the underlying LLM service/model weights are assumed uncompromised, and the OS kernel, cryptographic primitives, container sandboxes, and the Agent-BOM telemetry collection/storage infrastructure are all assumed secure and tamper-proof; collection hooks/adapters are trusted to faithfully gather evidence. Out of scope: proving semantic correctness of every reasoning step, guaranteeing optimal agent action selection (e.g. mitigating benign hallucination), and runtime enforcement/sandboxing/access control -- the paper is explicit that Agent-BOM is a post-hoc auditing/representation layer, not a preventive defense mechanism.
limitations: The authors' own Discussion section states the following explicitly: (1) Agent-BOM is an auditing representation, not a standalone defense or enforcement mechanism -- it does not replace runtime enforcement, sandboxing, or access control. (2) Its quality is bounded by instrumentation/observability completeness; frameworks that expose only coarse-grained logs or omit context assembly, memory read/write, tool invocation, or inter-agent communication will yield incomplete graphs that weaken path reconstruction and adjudication. (3) Long-term memory and multi-agent propagation remain the hardest cases in practice -- shared memory, asynchronous communication, task delegation, and external service chains lengthen propagation paths and complicate attribution; scaling requires future work on incremental graph maintenance, cross-session correlation, and cross-agent path pruning. (4) Audit completeness and adjudication accuracy remain bounded by evidence completeness and rule quality; missing instrumentation or inaccurate security attributes can produce partial paths, under-approximated risks, or ambiguous adjudication -- the framework is explicitly not a guarantee of complete detection or zero-false-positive judgment. Beyond the authors' own caveats: the evaluation covers only four hand-constructed composite scenarios on a single agent runtime (OpenClaw) with a single base model (GPT-5), with no quantitative detection metrics, no false-positive analysis, no comparison against an actual competing detection tool (only a qualitative representation-family comparison table), and the artifact repository is stated as to-be-released only upon acceptance, so the schema/rules/plugin were not independently verifiable at review time.

datasets_used: No standard benchmark dataset used; four self-constructed composite attack scenarios reproduced in OpenClaw from attack cases reported in prior work (cited as [40],[41],[42] in the paper)
quantitative_results: No quantitative metrics (precision/recall/F1/detection rate) are reported anywhere in the paper

Table II qualitative rubric: Agent-BOM is rated the only representation family with checkmark (direct/explicit/systematic support) on all six auditing questions Q1-Q6, 'High' representation-layer portability, and 'High' overall auditing support, versus SBOM/AIBOM (checkmark only on partial Q1, cross elsewhere, 'Limited' auditing support), Logging ('Limited'), Tracing/Observability/generic and agent-workflow provenance ('Partial'), reasoning provenance ('Partial', with direct support on Q2 and Q5 only), and security-oriented provenance ('Risk-specific', direct support on Q5 only)

All four case studies report qualitative per-phase adjudication labels (e.g., Memory Poisoning, Tool Misuse, Agentic Supply-Chain Vulnerability, Unexpected Code Execution, Agent Goal Hijacking, Insecure Inter-Agent Communication, Cascading Failures, Rogue Agent, Human-Agent Trust Exploitation, Identity and Privilege Abuse) rather than numeric scores
baselines_compared: No system-level baseline detector was run head-to-head; the only comparison is the qualitative representation-family rubric in Table II against SBOM, AIBOM, Logging, Tracing, Observability, generic provenance, agent/workflow provenance, reasoning provenance, and security-oriented provenance as representation *families* (not specific tools)
